summary: This lesson introduces the past participle for -er/-ir verbs (-ido), practices haber forms, and teaches the subject pronouns yo, él, ella, tú, usted, nosotros/nosotras, ellos/ellas, ustedes. The distinction between subject pronouns and object pronouns (two separate sets) is made explicit.

teachings.summary: For -er and -ir verbs, the past participle is formed by removing -er/-ir and adding -ido. This is the same for both verb groups — the only difference between -er and -ir verbs remains the 'we' present tense form.
teachings.rule: For -er/-ir verbs: remove -er or -ir, add -ido (vender → vendido, venir → venido, perder → perdido).

examples.english: to come → come (as in bienvenido = well-come)

teachings.notes: Bienvenido (welcome) comes from bien + venido = well-come.

teachings.summary: With haber + past participle, both are changed verbs, so object pronouns can only go before haber. There is no infinitive to attach them to.
teachings.rule: In haber constructions, the pronoun goes before haber: lo he terminado. After the infinitive or before the changed verb — here both verbs are changed, so only one position works.
teachings.notes: This is a rare situation with two changed verbs together. Usually after a changed verb comes a complete verb (quiero terminar).

teachings.conceptName: Subject pronouns: yo, tú, él, ella, usted, nosotros, ellos, ellas
teachings.summary: Subject pronouns are optional and used for emphasis or clarification. They go in a different position than object pronouns. Two separate sets of words: subject pronouns (yo, tú, él, ella, usted, nosotros/as, ellos/as) and object pronouns (me, te, lo, la, nos, los, las).
teachings.rule: Subject pronouns: yo (I), tú (you informal), él (he), ella (she), usted (you formal), nosotros/nosotras (we), ellos/ellas (they). These are optional, for emphasis/clarification. Object pronouns: me, te, lo, la, nos, los, las.
teachings.notes: Tú (subject, 'you see') vs te (object, 'I see you') — English doesn't distinguish these for 'you' but does for he/him, she/her. Nosotras is for an all-female group. Usted is a contraction of 'vuestra merced' (your honour). Ellos/ellas: double-l pronounced /y/ in most dialects.

teachings.conceptName: tú vs te: subject vs object 'you'
teachings.summary: Tú is the subject pronoun (you see = tú ves) while te is the object pronoun (I see you = te veo). English doesn't change 'you' in these positions, which can cause confusion for learners.
teachings.rule: tú goes with yo, él, ella (subject position). te goes with me, lo, la (object position).
